start.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start.text: The inn door opens and a man enters in black plate armor, polished to a mirror shine. Two soldiers flank him, hands on sword hilts. The room falls silent. He removes his gloves one finger at a time and surveys the room with pale grey eyes.

start_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_2.text: Captain Aldric Draven, Third Diplomatic Corps of the Ironmarch Legion. I represent General Harren and the will of the Iron Parliament. I've come to discuss the future of Ashvale.

start_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_3.text: He sits without being invited, his soldiers taking positions by the door. He folds his hands on the table like a man about to deliver a sermon.

start_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_4.text: The Legion has observed Ashvale's... difficulties. Crops failing. Factions feuding. A lord who won't leave his manor. You are a village waiting to be swallowed by the first strong wind. We are offering to be the wall against that wind.

start_5.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_5.text: Full integration into Legion territory. Your lord retains his title — ceremonially. A garrison of fifty soldiers stationed here for your protection. Trade routes opened to the east. In exchange: taxes, conscription quotas, and obedience to Legion law.

start_6.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_6.text: He pauses, letting the silence do its work. Then, almost gently:

start_7.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_7.text: You have seven days to accept. I'll be staying at the inn. The terms are generous, I assure you. I've seen what happens to villages that decline.

options.text: Ashvale can't afford to refuse. We accept your terms.
Those terms are a leash. Let's talk about something both sides can live with.
Ashvale has survived three hundred years without the Legion. We'll survive three hundred more.
[ATTACK] Draw your weapon. This diplomat came to the wrong village.
Seven days, you said? We'll need every one of them. Let us deliberate.

accepted.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
accepted.text: Draven's eyebrows rise — barely. He wasn't expecting that.

accepted_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
accepted_2.text: Wise. Most villages take the full seven days and still make the same choice. You've simply saved everyone the anxiety. I'll draft the formal terms tonight. Your lord will sign them in the morning.

accepted_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
accepted_3.text: He stands, pulls on his gloves. At the door, he pauses.

accepted_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
accepted_4.text: For what it's worth — the Legion isn't what the stories say. We bring order. Roads. Trade. Safety. The old way of doing things is dying. We're simply... hastening the inevitable.

negotiate_response.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_response.text: Draven tilts his head, studying you like a hawk studies a mouse that just stood on its hind legs.

negotiate_response_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_response_2.text: A leash. He almost smiles. I prefer 'partnership with clearly defined parameters.' But very well. You have something to say — say it. I was trained to listen before I was trained to fight.

options.text: Trade access, yes. Garrison, no. Ashvale governs itself, pays a tribute in grain and goods.
Before we discuss terms — tell me what the Legion really wants with a failing village in the middle of nowhere.
What if I could offer the Legion something more valuable than a village? I could be useful to you — on the inside.

negotiate_trade.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_trade.text: He drums his fingers on the table once. Twice.

negotiate_trade_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_trade_2.text: Tribute in lieu of garrison. Partial autonomy. The Legion gets its trade route and its taxes, and your lord keeps his title with actual authority instead of ceremonial. It's... not unprecedented.

negotiate_trade_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_trade_3.text: I can take this to General Harren. But understand — if you default on tribute, even once, the garrison comes. No second negotiation. And I'll need something to sweeten the offer. Information, perhaps. About the other powers in this region.

negotiate_truth.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_truth.text: Draven leans back. For the first time, his mask slips — just a fraction.

negotiate_truth_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_truth_2.text: The Thornwood road is the fastest route between the eastern provinces and the capital. Whoever controls Ashvale controls that road. Your village isn't the prize — it's the tollbooth.

negotiate_truth_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
negotiate_truth_3.text: And — I'll tell you this for free, because it serves me — you already have a problem you don't know about. Someone in your village has been sending reports to the Legion for months. Your factions, your weaknesses, your lord's secrets. I know things about Ashvale that would make your lord weep.

options.text: Who is the spy? Give me a name.
It doesn't matter who the spy is. What matters is your answer — will you negotiate fairly or not?

spy_response_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
spy_response_2.text: I protect my assets. But I'll tell you this: they're closer to the lord than you think. Draw your own conclusions.

betrayal_offer.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
betrayal_offer.text: Draven goes very still. His soldiers shift at the door. He waves them down without looking.

betrayal_offer_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
betrayal_offer_2.text: Now that is interesting. An outsider with no allegiance, embedded in a village's power structure, willing to work for the winning side. You're either very smart or very desperate.

betrayal_offer_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
betrayal_offer_3.text: Here's what I need: weaken the village's ability to resist. Sabotage the unity efforts. Keep the factions divided. When the Legion moves, I want Ashvale to fold like wet paper. Do this, and you'll have rank, land, and the General's personal favor.

betrayal_offer_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
betrayal_offer_4.text: Report to me nightly. Tell no one. And understand: if you betray the Legion, the Legion does not forgive. We don't need to. We simply outlast.

refused.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
refused.text: Draven regards you for a long moment. His expression doesn't change. If anything, he looks tired.

refused_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
refused_2.text: Three hundred years. That's what they always say. And then they see the Legion on the horizon and three hundred years of pride evaporates in about three minutes.

refused_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
refused_3.text: You still have seven days. My offer stands until then. After that... well. I'll be recalled, and the people who replace me don't carry quills. They carry siege engines.

refused_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
refused_4.text: He stands. His soldiers open the door.

refused_5.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
refused_5.text: I respect conviction, even misguided conviction. Use the seven days. Talk to your people. Think about what you're willing to sacrifice for the word 'freedom.' Because the Legion will ask you to pay for it — in full.

attack_response.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
attack_response.text: Draven moves faster than a man in plate armor should. His sword clears the scabbard before you've finished drawing yours. His soldiers level crossbows at your chest.

options.text: [FIGHT] Commit to the attack. Take Draven down.
...Lower your weapon. This was a mistake.

attack_backed_down.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
attack_backed_down.text: Draven sheathes his sword slowly. Deliberately. Making you watch.

attack_backed_down_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
attack_backed_down_2.text: That was remarkably stupid. I'm going to pretend it didn't happen — not for your sake, but because killing diplomats is bad for everyone's reputation, including mine. You now have six days instead of seven. Don't waste them.

stall_response.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
stall_response.text: Draven inclines his head slightly. A diplomat's nod — it commits to nothing.

stall_response_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
stall_response_2.text: Of course. Seven days is the standard period. I'll be here — observing, speaking with your people, getting to know this charming village. Don't worry, I'm quite pleasant company when I'm not delivering ultimatums.

stall_response_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
stall_response_3.text: He pauses at the door, looking back over his shoulder.

stall_response_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
stall_response_4.text: A word of advice, freely given: the longer you wait, the more your people will argue. Division is the real enemy here — not me. I'm just the messenger. What comes after me is... less articulate.
